title: Abbasid Revolution
summary: Between 747 and 750 a coalition of Persian, Arab and Khurasani grievances rallied under the black banners of the Prophet's Hashimite kin and destroyed the Umayyad caliphate. At the battle of the Great Zab in January 750 the last Umayyad caliph Marwan II was routed; within months his dynasty was hunted through Syria and Egypt to virtual extinction. The Abbasid family took the caliphate, moved its centre from Damascus toward Iraq, and inaugurated the political and cultural order that soon flourished in the new city of Baghdad.

sections.heading: Background
sections.body:
The Umayyad caliphate, ruling from Damascus since 661, had built the largest contiguous empire the Old World had yet seen, stretching from the Indus to the Pyrenees. Its Arab governors and armies had absorbed the wealth of Iran, Syria and Egypt, and its coinage and administrative reforms under Abd al-Malik around 700 had given the Islamic state a distinctive imperial identity. But the Umayyads were also, in the eyes of many of their subjects, a narrowly Arab and specifically Syrian dynasty, whose privileges rested on tribal client networks and whose taxation of non-Arab Muslim converts, or mawali, betrayed the ideal of a universal religious community.

Opposition accumulated on several fronts. Shi'ite partisans of the Prophet's family had never accepted the Umayyads, whose founder Muawiya had defeated the caliph Ali and whose grandson Yazid had killed Ali's son Husayn at Karbala in 680. Kharijite militants regarded Umayyad rule as apostasy. And in the eastern province of Khurasan, where Arab settlers, Iranian mawali and Persian aristocrats lived tightly interwoven, resentment against the fiscal impositions of governors sent from Damascus was acute. When the last strong Umayyad caliph Hisham ibn Abd al-Malik died in 743, a succession of short reigns opened by his nephew al-Walid II unleashed a third fitna, or civil war, that shook the whole Syrian core.

Into this vacuum stepped the Hashimiyya, a secretive movement centred on the descendants of the Prophet's uncle Abbas ibn Abd al-Muttalib. Cleverly, the family did not at first press its own name but called simply for a leader al-rida min al Muhammad, one acceptable from the house of Muhammad, a formula broad enough to attract Alid partisans, disaffected Iranians and anti-Umayyad Arabs alike. From the town of Humayma in southern Jordan the imam Ibrahim ibn Muhammad directed a network of missionaries across the empire. Their key agent in the east was Abu Muslim al-Khurasani, a young freedman of obscure Persian origin whose organisational genius and personal ruthlessness would become legendary.

On 15 June 747, at the town of Safidhanj near Merv in Khurasan, Abu Muslim raised the black banner that would become the movement's emblem. It was said to symbolise mourning for the martyred house of the Prophet, but it also served the practical purpose of distinguishing the rebels from the white banners of the Umayyads. Within a year the Khurasani army, disciplined and increasingly confident, had defeated the Umayyad governor Nasr ibn Sayyar and taken Merv, then swept westwards along the Silk Road into Iran. Marwan II, the vigorous but harried last Umayyad caliph, was already occupied with rebellions in Syria and Iraq.

sections.heading: What happened
sections.body:
In August or September 749 the Khurasani vanguard under Qahtaba ibn Shabib crossed the Zagros and defeated the Umayyad general Ibn Hubayra outside Nihavand. Qahtaba himself was killed as his men forced the Tigris, but his son al-Hasan pressed on and took Kufa in southern Iraq without a fight. Kufa was the ancestral base of Alid loyalty and the natural revolutionary capital. On 28 November 749 a crowd in the great mosque acclaimed Abu al-Abbas Abd Allah ibn Muhammad, a great-great-grandson of the Prophet's uncle, as caliph. He took the regnal title al-Saffah, sometimes translated as the Blood-Shedder, and made clear that the vague call for a leader from the house of Muhammad now meant, quite specifically, an Abbasid.

Marwan II gathered what troops he could and marched east from his base in northern Syria. On 25 January 750, on the banks of the Great Zab river, a left-bank tributary of the Tigris in what is now Iraqi Kurdistan, the two armies met. Al-Saffah's uncle Abd Allah ibn Ali commanded the Abbasid forces. Marwan's Syrian veterans, some sixty thousand strong in the Arab estimates, had lost none of their fighting quality, but they were exhausted from years of internal war and their morale had begun to crack. The Khurasanis, in disciplined ranks of spearmen and mounted archers, absorbed the Umayyad charge and then closed. Marwan's line broke; thousands drowned trying to swim the Zab; the caliph himself fled.

The pursuit was relentless. Marwan was chased through Harran, then across Palestine into Egypt, and finally cornered in a Coptic church at Busir in the Nile delta, where he was killed in early August 750. The Abbasids meanwhile set about extirpating the rest of the Umayyad family. In the notorious feast at Nahr Abi Futrus in Palestine, sometime in June 750, Abd Allah ibn Ali invited about eighty Umayyad princes to a banquet under promise of amnesty, then had them clubbed to death; the servants were said to have finished the meal on carpets thrown over the bodies. Umayyad graves as far back as Muawiya himself were desecrated. One prince escaped: the twenty-year-old Abd al-Rahman ibn Muawiya, grandson of caliph Hisham, slipped out of Syria, crossed North Africa disguised in a Berber cloak, and in 756 made himself amir of al-Andalus at Cordoba, founding a rival Umayyad state that would outlive Baghdad's Abbasids by three centuries.

sections.heading: Aftermath & consequences
sections.body:
The Abbasid takeover was more than a change of dynasty. Al-Saffah's brief reign, cut short by smallpox in 754, was mostly consumed with consolidation, but his brother and successor al-Mansur, who ruled until 775, made the transformation permanent. He moved the capital away from Damascus and its Syrian tribal politics, first to a series of encampments in Iraq and then, in 762, to a new round city on the west bank of the Tigris that he named Madinat al-Salam, the City of Peace, though everyone called it Baghdad. Within a generation Baghdad had become the largest city west of Chang'an, drawing scholars, merchants and craftsmen from across the caliphate.

The political style of the new regime borrowed heavily from Persian imperial models. Abbasid caliphs were remote figures, seldom appearing in public, screened by an elaborate court of chamberlains and secretaries. The most influential of these officials belonged to the Barmakid family, whose grandfather had been a Buddhist high priest at Balkh; under Harun al-Rashid the Barmakids effectively ran the empire until their spectacular fall in 803. Iranian bureaucrats staffed the diwans; Arab tribal armies gave way to a paid, mostly Khurasani, later Turkish, standing army. The reforms taken together lifted the mawali out of second-class status and gave Islamic civilisation a genuinely trans-cultural cast.

Relations with the losing party were not so easily settled. Abu Muslim, indispensable to the revolution, was becoming inconvenient to it, and in 755 al-Mansur summoned him to court and had him assassinated. The killing provoked immediate revolts in Khurasan, most seriously the movement of Sunpad the Magian, and set a pattern of tension between Baghdad and the eastern provinces that would eventually spawn the effectively independent Tahirid, Saffarid and Samanid dynasties. In the west, Abd al-Rahman I's Andalusi Umayyad state was a permanent embarrassment. And the Shi'ite groups that had rallied under the vague slogan of the house of Muhammad, only to find themselves ruled by an Abbasid rather than an Alid caliph, would spend the next two centuries generating rebellions, from the Zaydi imams of Tabaristan to the Isma'ili Fatimids of Ifriqiya.

sections.heading: Legacy
sections.body:
The Abbasid revolution set the political shape of the medieval Islamic world. For half a millennium, until the Mongol sack of Baghdad in 1258, the Abbasid caliphate served as the notional overlordship under which regional sultanates, from the Ghaznavids in Afghanistan to the Fatimids in Egypt to the Buyids in Iran, defined themselves. Even after 1258, when the last effective Abbasid was killed by Hulagu's soldiers, a client Abbasid family was maintained at Cairo by the Mamluks as a source of dynastic legitimacy until the Ottoman conquest of 1517. The idea that legitimate rule required the blessing of a caliph descended from the Prophet's family, articulated by the revolution, has cast a long shadow into modern Muslim politics.

Culturally the revolution's most enduring consequence was the translation movement that got under way once Baghdad became the empire's centre. Greek, Persian, Syriac and Sanskrit works of philosophy, medicine, astronomy and mathematics were rendered into Arabic under Abbasid patronage, especially in the reign of al-Ma'mun in the early ninth century, giving Islamic civilisation a scientific inheritance that would in turn seed the European renaissance. The Persianate style of court, poetry and administration that the Abbasids inaugurated would be echoed as far east as Mughal India and as far west as Ottoman Istanbul.

Modern historians differ over how far to see the revolution as a genuine social upheaval, a coup by a dissident Arab faction, or a Persian re-conquest of a temporarily Arabic empire. Marshall Hodgson influentially called it a shift from an Arab kingdom to an Islamic commonwealth. Whatever the label, the events of 747 to 750 unmistakably closed one chapter of Muslim history and opened another. The Umayyad Arab imperium was gone, and in its place stood a self-consciously universal, Persianate, cosmopolitan empire ruled from a new capital in Iraq.

worldContext.text: In Francia the mayor of the palace Pippin the Short had, with papal blessing, deposed the last Merovingian king Childeric III in 751 and had himself crowned the first Carolingian ruler at Soissons. His son Charlemagne was still a small child. In Anglo-Saxon Britain the missionary Boniface, an English monk operating in Germany, was murdered at Dokkum in Frisia in 754 while working to convert the Frisians. The Iconoclast emperor Constantine V ruled a diminished but energetic Byzantium from Constantinople.

In China the Tang dynasty under the long-reigning Emperor Xuanzong was drifting toward the disaster that broke in December 755 with the An Lushan Rebellion, only five years after the Zab. In July 751 a Tang army was defeated by an Abbasid army at the Talas river in modern Kyrgyzstan, a border clash better remembered by later historians than by contemporaries but conventionally credited with transmitting the Chinese secret of paper-making westwards into the Islamic world.

Berber Ibadi Kharijite movements had shaken Umayyad North Africa through the 740s and were carving out effectively autonomous polities in the Maghreb. In sub-Saharan Africa the trans-Saharan gold trade was gaining momentum, with the Sanhaja Berber town of Awdaghust and the emerging Wagadu (Ghana) kingdom of the Soninke acting as hinges between the Mediterranean world and the West African savanna. In Christian Nubia the Baqt treaty with the Arabs continued to protect Makurian independence.

In Mesoamerica the great city of Teotihuacan had collapsed around 550 and its former hinterland was now divided among successor polities such as Cholula and Xochicalco. In the Maya lowlands the Late Classic cities were at their peak, though the calamitous cycle of drought and warfare that would depopulate the southern lowlands was less than a century away. On the Peruvian coast the Moche were finished and the Wari empire was reaching its greatest extent through the highlands.

The great Polynesian voyages of colonisation into eastern Polynesia were under way. Hawai'i was probably first settled around this time by canoes navigating from the Marquesas and Society Islands, using stars, swells and bird flight as guides. In New Zealand the Maori landings still lay several centuries in the future, but the Society Islands and Cook Islands were already densely populated and building the marae stone platforms that mark the Polynesian ritual landscape.

figures.name: Abu al-Abbas al-Saffah
figures.role: First Abbasid caliph, acclaimed at Kufa on 28 November 749; reigned until his death from smallpox in 754

figures.name: Abu Muslim al-Khurasani
figures.role: Chief organiser of the revolt in Khurasan; raised the black banner in June 747 and was assassinated by al-Mansur in 755

figures.name: Marwan II
figures.role: Last Umayyad caliph, ruled from 744; defeated at the Great Zab in January 750 and killed at Busir in Egypt six months later

figures.name: Abd Allah ibn Ali
figures.role: Uncle of al-Saffah and Abbasid commander at the Great Zab; organised the massacre of Umayyad princes at Nahr Abi Futrus

figures.name: Abd al-Rahman I
figures.role: Umayyad prince who escaped the massacre and founded the Emirate of Cordoba in 756

images.caption: The Islamic empire on the eve of the Abbasid revolution stretched from the Atlantic to the Indus; the map shows the successive phases of expansion from 622 to 750.
Modern statue of Abd al-Rahman I at Almunecar in Spain, where the fugitive Umayyad prince is said to have landed in 755 on his way to founding the Emirate of Cordoba.
images.credit: Photograph by NoelWalley, via Wikimedia Commons

sources.title: Abu Muslim: The Architect of the Abbasid Revolution
